source_paper_title: How Many Vertices Does a Random Walk Miss in a Network with a Moderately Increasing Number of Vertices?

A natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) for any RWoGG \((d,(G(i))_{i\ge 1},(P^{(i)})_{i\ge 1})\).

title: Characterize when constant expected unvisited vertices requires duration at least hitting time
background_latex:
Consider a growing graph process in discrete time in which, for each integer 
\(i\ge 1\), there is a connected simple undirected graph \(G^{(i)}=(V^{(i)},E^{(i)})\) with \(V^{(i)}=\{v_1,\dots,v_i\}\) and \(G^{(i+1)}\) is obtained from \(G^{(i)}\) by adding one new vertex \(v_{i+1}\) and connecting it to at least one existing vertex (so \(V^{(i)}\subseteq V^{(i+1)}\)).

Let \(d:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) be a duration (growth-rate) function: the system stays on \(G^{(i)}\) for exactly \(d(i)\) consecutive random-walk steps before the next vertex is added. Define the change times \(T_1:=0\) and \(T_{i}:=\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} d(j)\) for \(i\ge 2\), so that the vertex set size equals \(i\) during times \(t\in[T_i,T_{i+1})\).

A random walk on this growing graph is specified by transition matrices \(P^{(i)}\in[0,1]^{V^{(i)}\times V^{(i)}}\), where during the period when the current graph is \(G^{(i)}\), the walker evolves as the time-homogeneous Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\). Assume each \(P^{(i)}\) is irreducible (so the walk on \(G^{(i)}\) is well-defined) and define its hitting time
\[
 t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i) \, := \, \max_{u,v\in V^{(i)}} \mathbb{E}\big[\min\{t\ge 0: X_0=u,\ X_t=v\}\big],
\]
where \((X_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denotes a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\).

Let \((Z_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denote the overall walk on the growing graph (so \(Z_t\in V_t\), and in fact \(Z_t\in V_{t-1}\) for \(t\ge 1\)). Define the number of unvisited vertices just before the \((n+1)\)-st vertex is added by
\[
U(n) \, := \, \left|\, V^{(n)} \setminus \bigcup_{t=0}^{T_{n+1}} \{Z_t\}\,\right|,
\]
and write \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) for its expectation under the law of the walk determined by \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following implication holds uniformly over all random walks on growing graphs as defined above:

If \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)] = O(1)\) as \(n\to\infty\), must it be the case that
\[
 d(i) = \Omega\big(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\big) \quad \text{as } i\to\infty?
\]
Equivalently, decide whether there exists a family \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\) such that \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) stays bounded while \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to 0\), or prove that no such family exists.
latex:
Consider a growing graph process in discrete time in which, for each integer 
\(i\ge 1\), there is a connected simple undirected graph \(G^{(i)}=(V^{(i)},E^{(i)})\) with \(V^{(i)}=\{v_1,\dots,v_i\}\) and \(G^{(i+1)}\) is obtained from \(G^{(i)}\) by adding one new vertex \(v_{i+1}\) and connecting it to at least one existing vertex (so \(V^{(i)}\subseteq V^{(i+1)}\)).

Let \(d:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}\) be a duration (growth-rate) function: the system stays on \(G^{(i)}\) for exactly \(d(i)\) consecutive random-walk steps before the next vertex is added. Define the change times \(T_1:=0\) and \(T_{i}:=\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} d(j)\) for \(i\ge 2\), so that the vertex set size equals \(i\) during times \(t\in[T_i,T_{i+1})\).

A random walk on this growing graph is specified by transition matrices \(P^{(i)}\in[0,1]^{V^{(i)}\times V^{(i)}}\), where during the period when the current graph is \(G^{(i)}\), the walker evolves as the time-homogeneous Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\). Assume each \(P^{(i)}\) is irreducible (so the walk on \(G^{(i)}\) is well-defined) and define its hitting time
\[
 t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i) \, := \, \max_{u,v\in V^{(i)}} \mathbb{E}\big[\min\{t\ge 0: X_0=u,\ X_t=v\}\big],
\]
where \((X_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denotes a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P^{(i)}\).

Let \((Z_t)_{t\ge 0}\) denote the overall walk on the growing graph (so \(Z_t\in V_t\), and in fact \(Z_t\in V_{t-1}\) for \(t\ge 1\)). Define the number of unvisited vertices just before the \((n+1)\)-st vertex is added by
\[
U(n) \, := \, \left|\, V^{(n)} \setminus \bigcup_{t=0}^{T_{n+1}} \{Z_t\}\,\right|,
\]
and write \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) for its expectation under the law of the walk determined by \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\).

Determine whether the following implication holds uniformly over all random walks on growing graphs as defined above:

If \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)] = O(1)\) as \(n\to\infty\), must it be the case that
\[
 d(i) = \Omega\big(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\big) \quad \text{as } i\to\infty?
\]
Equivalently, decide whether there exists a family \((d,(G^{(i)}),(P^{(i)}))\) such that \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) stays bounded while \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to 0\), or prove that no such family exists.
location_in_paper: Page 5 (end of Section 1.2, discussion after Theorem 1.3) and Page 23, Section 5 Concluding Remarks.
open_problem_quote:
A natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) for any RWoGG \((d,(G(i))_{i\ge 1},(P^{(i)})_{i\ge 1})\).

A general lower bound of \(E[U]\) is a challenge: a natural question remains unsettled whether \(E[U] = O(1)\) requires \(d(i) = \Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample would amount to constructing growing graphs and transition rules with provably small \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) despite durations \(d(i)\ll t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\). Candidate families can be searched computationally on small \(n\) by simulating \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) and estimating hitting times, but verifying asymptotic separation and ruling out finite-size artifacts is nontrivial.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate constructions (graph-growth rules, attachment choices, and possibly transition matrices) and use simulation-based fitness such as small estimated \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) penalized by large estimated \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)/d(i)\). This provides an automated feedback signal, but the search space is large and the fitness proxy is noisy and may not correlate with the asymptotic statement.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper introduces the RWoGG model and proves strong sufficient conditions for bounded expected number of unvisited vertices: uniformly over all irreducible chains on each \(G^{(i)}\), \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]=O(1)\) holds if \(d(i)\ge C\,t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) for some constant \(C>1\), and \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\to 0\) if \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to\infty\). It also provides refined sufficient conditions under additional assumptions (lazy/reversible, control of \(t_{\mathrm{mix}}\), or controlled edge growth) and matching lower bounds for specific families (complete graphs and paths), but it leaves open whether the \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}\)-scale is necessary in full generality.

Existing literature on cover times and dynamic graphs supplies background techniques but does not resolve the necessity direction. Classical results (e.g., Matthews' method) relate cover time to \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}\log n\) on static graphs, while work on evolving graphs with fixed vertex sets (Avin–Koucký–Lotker; Sauerwald–Zanetti) shows that time-inhomogeneity can substantially alter hitting/cover behavior, suggesting that proving a universal lower bound in terms of \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}\) may require additional structural assumptions. Tools for time-inhomogeneous Markov chains (Saloff-Coste–Zúñiga) and refined inequalities for reversible chains (Oliveira–Peres) appear promising for either constructing counterexamples where \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) is large but new vertices are nevertheless discovered quickly, or for proving that bounded \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) forces a uniform per-round success probability that in turn implies \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) under mild regularity.

At present, with no forward-citation evidence of a resolution and given that the source paper (2020) explicitly states the question as unsettled, the problem should be regarded as open.

sources.title: How Many Vertices Does a Random Walk Miss in a Network with Moderately Increasing the Number of Vertices?
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the random walk on a growing graph (RWoGG) model with duration function \(d(i)\) and studies the expected number \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) of unvisited vertices at change times. Proves general sufficient conditions: if \(d(i)\ge C\,t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\) for some constant \(C>1\), then \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]=O(1)\); if \(d(i)/t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\to\infty\), then \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\to 0\). Provides tight bounds for growing complete graphs and growing paths, and explicitly leaves open whether \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]=O(1)\) forces \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\) in full generality.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether bounded expected unvisited vertices implies durations at least on the order of the hitting time uniformly over all growing-graph random walks.

sources.title: Covering problems for Markov chains
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops inequalities relating cover time to hitting times, including the classical bound \(t_{\mathrm{cov}}\le t_{\mathrm{hit}}\log n\) (up to constants) via what is now called Matthews' method.
sources.connection: The source paper notes that a naive sufficient condition \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\log i)\) follows from exponential decay of unvisited vertices; Matthews' method is part of the static-graph background linking hitting and covering, motivating the question of whether \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}\) alone is the right scale in the growing setting.

sources.title: Crawling on simple models of web graphs
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies random walks on preferential-attachment web graph models where vertices are added over time; shows that when growth is constant-rate, the proportion of vertices visited converges to a constant (so a linear number remain unvisited).
sources.connection: Provides a contrasting regime (constant duration) where \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) is linear, motivating the need for duration conditions; however it does not resolve whether bounded \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) forces \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\).

sources.title: Cover time and mixing time of random walks on dynamic graphs
sources.theorem_or_result: Analyzes cover and mixing times for random walks on evolving graphs with fixed vertex set; provides upper/lower bounds and examples where dynamics can dramatically increase cover time.
sources.connection: Although the vertex set is static (unlike RWoGG), techniques and phenomena about time-inhomogeneous walks inform what can go wrong when the environment changes, relevant to constructing counterexamples or proving necessity of \(d(i)=\Omega(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i))\).

sources.title: Random walks on dynamic graphs: Mixing times, hitting times, and return probabilities
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides bounds for mixing, hitting, and return probabilities for random walks on dynamic graphs under assumptions such as common stationary distribution; includes cover-time consequences for regular dynamic graphs.
sources.connection: Relevant methodological background: relates dynamic-graph walk behavior to hitting/mixing parameters, potentially useful for attacking whether bounded \(\mathbb{E}[U(n)]\) enforces a hitting-time-scale duration in the growing-vertex model.

sources.title: Random walks on graphs: new bounds on hitting, meeting, coalescing and returning
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes sharp inequalities connecting hitting/meeting/coalescence times for reversible chains; provides tools to compare these quantities and derive bounds in terms of stationary distribution and spectral gap.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for necessity of \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}\)-scale durations; refined relationships among hitting/mixing/return times may help either prove necessity or build counterexamples where \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}\) is large but typical discovery of new vertices is fast.

sources.title: Merging for time inhomogeneous finite Markov chains. I. Singular values and stability
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a framework for convergence/merging of time-inhomogeneous Markov chains using singular values and stability conditions, giving quantitative bounds on distance to evolving stationary measures.
sources.connection: RWoGG is a piecewise-homogeneous but globally time-inhomogeneous chain; merging/stability tools may help control visitation probabilities across rounds when \(d(i)\) is below \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\), relevant to proving necessity or finding counterexamples.

sources.title: Merging for inhomogeneous finite Markov chains part II: Nash and log-Sobolev inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Extends merging theory for inhomogeneous chains using Nash and log-Sobolev inequalities, providing conditions for uniform convergence rates under time variation.
sources.connection: Potentially applicable to bounding unvisited mass in the growing-graph walk by controlling how quickly distributions 'merge' within each round and across rounds, which is central to relating \(d(i)\) to intrinsic timescales like \(t_{\mathrm{hit}}(i)\).
